title: The Explosive Diarrhea Outbreak Is About to Get Much Bigger
gist: A fast-spreading and likely undercounted US cyclosporiasis outbreak (nearly 7,000 reported cases, 3,300+ in Michigan) tied to tainted lettuce is expected to widen nationally because the parasite resists chlorine and common sanitizers and can spread through irrigation water into the food-distribution network.

insights.claim: US cyclosporiasis case counts (nearly 7,000 nationally, over 3,300 in Michigan as of mid-July 2026) are likely at least half the true total because the illness is underdiagnosed and not included in standard gastrointestinal disease testing panels, according to IDSA CEO Jeanne Marrazzo.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
entities: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
insights.so_what: Official CDC outbreak figures understate true prevalence, meaning downstream liability, recall scope, and demand for foodborne-pathogen testing are all larger than headline numbers suggest.
insights.quote: That’s because of underdiagnosis and also because there are cases that are likely to be mild. Many people aren’t going to declare themselves and get counted.
insights.locator: The actual case count is almost certainly higher

insights.claim: Cyclospora oocysts lodge into the crevices of fresh produce and resist bleach, common commercial sanitizers, and chlorine (the primary disinfectant in most municipal water/wastewater systems), meaning standard produce washing does not reliably eliminate the parasite.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: A food-safety control gap exists for pathogens resistant to bleach/chlorine, pointing to unmet demand for alternative produce-sanitizing or irrigation-water-treatment technology.
insights.quote: Despite the commercial approaches to washing the produce that ends up in our grocery stores, the oocysts can continue to stick.
insights.locator: Norman Beatty, associate professor of medicine

themes.theme: Irrigation water as the systemic vector

insights.claim: Food-poisoning lawyer Bill Marler says outbreaks are more likely caused by contaminated irrigation water supplies than by individual farmworker hygiene lapses, and rising infection counts increase the odds that infected feces recontaminate water used to irrigate crops, creating a self-reinforcing outbreak cycle.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Irrigation-water testing and treatment for produce growers is an under-addressed liability and infrastructure investment gap that recurring outbreaks will keep exposing.
insights.quote: Likely what happens is, it gets into the water supply that’s utilized to irrigate crops.
insights.locator: He adds that agricultural workers typically get blamed

insights.claim: Most historical cyclosporiasis outbreaks in the US were linked to imported produce, but the last decade produced the first purely domestic ("All-American") outbreaks, including a bagged-lettuce outbreak from an Illinois plant that sickened more than 700 people.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Domestic US produce supply chains have become a real cyclospora vector, not just imports, implying rising domestic ag food-safety compliance costs and traceability needs.
insights.locator: the last decade has seen the US’s first All-American cyclospora outbreaks

insights.claim: Taco Bell voluntarily and temporarily removed unspecified ingredients at select restaurants as a precaution against the cyclosporiasis outbreak, illustrating how restaurant chains respond to produce-safety scares with narrow, PR-driven supply actions.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Quick-service chains reliant on fresh lettuce/herbs face recurring reputational and menu-disruption risk during produce-safety outbreak seasons.
insights.quote: voluntarily and temporarily removed limited ingredients at select restaurants as a precautionary measure

insights.claim: University of Florida infectious disease specialist Norman Beatty predicts the current cyclosporiasis outbreak could end up being reported in all 50 US states because the national food-distribution network can move contaminated produce to many locations quickly.
insights.speaker: Emily Mullin
insights.so_what: Signals potential for a nationwide multi-state public-health and recall event this summer affecting fresh produce growers and quick-service restaurant supply chains.
insights.quote: This could end up being reported in all 50 states.
insights.locator: This shows us how easily an organism can be distributed

top_entities: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
